You are a programming expert. 
You will be provided with a patch used to fix a bug. 

Your task is summarize the modifications made in the patch.
Your summary should consist of two parts, modifications in each file and their relationships.

From b6060be66b6cbf447c0c62e5b80caa565e10f383 Mon Sep 17 00:00:00 2001
From: Jens Schauder <jschauder@pivotal.io>
Date: Thu, 28 Mar 2019 17:27:11 +0100
Subject: [PATCH] DATAJPA-1519 - Made the escapeCharacter itself available in
 SpEL expression.

Adapted documentation to reflect the changed API for escaping.

diff --git a/src/main/asciidoc/jpa.adoc b/src/main/asciidoc/jpa.adoc
index 54d239c73..5cffcd27a 100644
--- a/src/main/asciidoc/jpa.adoc
+++ b/src/main/asciidoc/jpa.adoc
@@ -126,6 +126,7 @@ The JPA module supports defining a query manually as a String or having it being
 Derived queries with the predicates `IsStartingWith`, `StartingWith`, `StartsWith`, IsEndingWith", `EndingWith`, `EndsWith`,
 `IsNotContaining`, `NotContaining`, `NotContains`, `IsContaining`, `Containing`, `Contains` the respective arguments for these queries will get sanitized.
 This means if the arguments actually contain characters recognized by `LIKE` as wildcards these will get escaped so they match only as literals.
+The escape character used can be configured by setting the `escapeCharacter` of the `@EnableJpaRepositories` annotation.
 Compare with <<jpa.query.spel-expressions>>.
 
 ==== Declared Queries
@@ -459,7 +460,7 @@ List<User> findByLastnameWithSpelExpression(@Param("lastname") String lastname);
 ====
 
 When using `like`-conditions with values that are coming from a not secure source the values should be sanitized so they can't contain any wildcards and thereby allow attackers to select more data than they should be able to.
-For this purpose the the `escape(String, String)` method is made available in the SpEL context.
+For this purpose the the `escape(String)` method is made available in the SpEL context.
 It prefixes all instances of `_` and `%` in the first argument with the single character from the second argument.
 In combination with the `escape` clause of the `like` expression available in JPQL and standard SQL this allows easy cleaning of bind parameters.
 
@@ -468,13 +469,14 @@ In combination with the `escape` clause of the `like` expression available in JP
 ====
 [source, java]
 ----
-@Query("select u from User u where u.firstname like %?#{#escape([0],'#')}% escape '#'")
+@Query("select u from User u where u.firstname like %?#{escape([0])}% escape ?#{escapeCharacter()}")
 List<User> findContainingEscaped(String namePart);
 ----
 ====
 
 Given this method declaration in an repository interface `findContainingEscaped("Peter_")" will find `Peter_Parker` but not `Peter Parker`.
-Note that the method `escape(String, String)` available in the SpEL context will only escape the SQL and JPQL standard wildcards `_` and `%`.
+The escape character used can be configured by setting the `escapeCharacter` of the `@EnableJpaRepositories` annotation.
+Note that the method `escape(String)` available in the SpEL context will only escape the SQL and JPQL standard wildcards `_` and `%`.
 If the underlying database or the JPA implementation supports additional wildcards these will not get escaped.
 
 [[jpa.modifying-queries]]

diff --git a/src/main/java/org/springframework/data/jpa/repository/query/EscapeCharacter.java b/src/main/java/org/springframework/data/jpa/repository/query/EscapeCharacter.java
index 269fa44ae..7d382a4d0 100644
--- a/src/main/java/org/springframework/data/jpa/repository/query/EscapeCharacter.java
+++ b/src/main/java/org/springframework/data/jpa/repository/query/EscapeCharacter.java
@@ -20,7 +20,7 @@ import lombok.Value;
 import java.util.Arrays;
 import java.util.List;
 
-import org.springframework.util.Assert;
+import org.springframework.lang.Nullable;
 
 /**
  * A value type encapsulating an escape character for LIKE queries and the actually usage of it in escaping
@@ -39,14 +39,25 @@ public class EscapeCharacter {
 	/**
 	 * Escapes all special like characters ({@code _}, {@code %}) using the configured escape character.
 	 *
-	 * @param value must not be {@literal null}.
+	 * @param value May be {@literal null}.
 	 * @return
 	 */
+	@Nullable
 	public String escape(String value) {
 
-		Assert.notNull(value, "Value must be not null.");
-
+		if (value == null) {
+			return null;
+		}
 		return TO_REPLACE.stream() //
 				.reduce(value, (it, character) -> it.replace(character, this.value + character));
 	}
+
+	/**
+	 * Makes the underlying character available.
+	 *
+	 * @return the value
+	 */
+	public char escapeCharacter() {
+		return value;
+	}
 }

diff --git a/src/test/java/org/springframework/data/jpa/repository/sample/UserRepository.java b/src/test/java/org/springframework/data/jpa/repository/sample/UserRepository.java
index a4216a539..30d3aa335 100644
--- a/src/test/java/org/springframework/data/jpa/repository/sample/UserRepository.java
+++ b/src/test/java/org/springframework/data/jpa/repository/sample/UserRepository.java
@@ -557,7 +557,7 @@ public interface UserRepository
 	List<NameOnlyDto> findByNamedQueryWithConstructorExpression();
 
 	// DATAJPA-1519
-	@Query("select u from User u where u.firstname like %?#{escape([0])}% escape '\\'")
+	@Query("select u from User u where u.firstname like %?#{escape([0])}% escape ?#{escapeCharacter()}")
 	List<User> findContainingEscaped(String namePart);
 
 	interface RolesAndFirstname {
-- 
2.34.1

### Modifications in Each File

1. **`src/main/asciidoc/jpa.adoc`**:
   - Added a note about configuring the `escapeCharacter` in the `@EnableJpaRepositories` annotation.
   - Updated the description of the `escape` method in the SpEL context to reflect that it now only takes one argument (`escape(String)` instead of `escape(String, String)`).
   - Modified the example query to use the new `escapeCharacter()` method instead of a hardcoded escape character.

2. **`src/main/java/org/springframework/data/jpa/repository/query/EscapeCharacter.java`**:
   - Changed the import statement from `org.springframework.util.Assert` to `org.springframework.lang.Nullable`.
   - Updated the `escape` method to allow `null` values and return `null` if the input is `null`.
   - Added a new method `escapeCharacter()` that returns the configured escape character.

3. **`src/test/java/org/springframework/data/jpa/repository/sample/UserRepository.java`**:
   - Updated the query in the `findContainingEscaped` method to use `?#{escapeCharacter()}` instead of a hardcoded escape character.

### Relationships Between Modifications

The modifications across the files are interconnected as follows:

- The documentation in `jpa.adoc` reflects the changes made in the `EscapeCharacter` class and the `UserRepository` interface, specifically regarding the new API for escaping characters. 
- The change in the `EscapeCharacter` class to allow `null` values and to provide a method for retrieving the escape character directly supports the updated query in the `UserRepository`, which now dynamically uses the configured escape character instead of a hardcoded value.
- The overall goal of these changes is to enhance the flexibility and security of query handling by allowing users to configure the escape character, which is crucial for preventing SQL injection attacks when using `LIKE` conditions.

Based on your summary and the commit message in the patch, you need to:

1. analyze the root cause of the bug in detail. 
2. output the file name that is most likely to cause the bug. 



Provide a response strictly in the following format:
Root cause:<Root cause>
file:<file name>

1. analyze the root cause of the bug in detail. 
2. output the file name that is most likely to cause the bug. 



Provide a response strictly in the following format:
Root cause:<Root cause>
file:<file name>

You are a programming expert. 
You will be provided with a patch used to fix a bug. 

Your task is summarize the modifications made in the patch.
Your summary should consist of two parts, modifications in each file and their relationships.

-For this purpose the the `escape(String, String)` method is made available in the SpEL context.

  * A value type encapsulating an escape character for LIKE queries and the actually usage of it in escaping
@@ -39,14 +39,25 @@ public class EscapeCharacter {
 	/**
 	 * Escapes all special like characters ({@code _}, {@code %}) using the configured escape character.
 	 *

 	 */

+		if (value == null) {

diff --git a/src/test/java/org/springframework/data/jpa/repository/sample/UserRepository.java b/src/test/java/org/springframework/data/jpa/repository/sample/UserRepository.java
index a4216a539..30d3aa335 100644
--- a/src/test/java/org/springframework/data/jpa/repository/sample/UserRepository.java
+++ b/src/test/java/org/springframework/data/jpa/repository/sample/UserRepository.java
@@ -557,7 +557,7 @@ public interface UserRepository
 	List<NameOnlyDto> findByNamedQueryWithConstructorExpression();
 
 	// DATAJPA-1519
-	@Query("select u from User u where u.firstname like %?#{escape([0])}% escape '\\'")
+	@Query("select u from User u where u.firstname like %?#{escape([0])}% escape ?#{escapeCharacter()}")
 	List<User> findContainingEscaped(String namePart);
 
 	interface RolesAndFirstname {
-- 
2.34.1

### Modifications in Each File

1. **`src/main/asciidoc/jpa.adoc`**:
   - Updated documentation to clarify that the `escapeCharacter` can be configured via the `@EnableJpaRepositories` annotation.
   - Changed the description of the `escape` method in the SpEL context from `escape(String, String)` to `escape(String)`, indicating that it now only takes one argument.
   - Adjusted the example query to use `?#{escapeCharacter()}` instead of a hardcoded escape character.

2. **`src/main/java/org/springframework/data/jpa/repository/query/EscapeCharacter.java`**:
   - Modified the `escape` method to allow `null` values, changing the documentation accordingly.
   - Added a new method `escapeCharacter()` that returns the configured escape character.

3. **`src/test/java/org/springframework/data/jpa/repository/sample/UserRepository.java`**:
   - Updated the query in the `findContainingEscaped` method to use `?#{escapeCharacter()}` instead of a hardcoded escape character.

### Relationships Between Modifications

The modifications across the files are interconnected as follows:

- The documentation in `jpa.adoc` reflects the changes made in the `EscapeCharacter` class, specifically the introduction of the `escapeCharacter()` method and the adjustment of the `escape` method to accept only one argument. This ensures that users of the API are aware of the new configuration options and method signatures.
- The changes in the `UserRepository` class demonstrate the practical application of the new `escapeCharacter()` method, replacing hardcoded values with a dynamic reference to the configured escape character. This aligns with the updated documentation and enhances the flexibility and security of the query construction.
- Overall, the modifications enhance the API's usability by allowing for a configurable escape character while ensuring that the documentation and implementation are consistent with these changes.

+++ b/src/main/asciidoc/jpa.adoc
@@ -126,6 +126,7 @@ The JPA module supports defining a query manually as a String or having it being

-- 
2.34.1

### Modifications in Each File

1. **EscapeCharacter.java**
   - Changed the import statement from `org.springframework.util.Assert` to `org.springframework.lang.Nullable`.
   - Updated the `escape` method to allow `null` values, removing the assertion that the value must not be null.
   - Added a new method `escapeCharacter()` that returns the underlying escape character.

2. **jpa.adoc**
   - Updated documentation to reflect the new API changes, specifically mentioning that the escape character can be configured using the `escapeCharacter` property in the `@EnableJpaRepositories` annotation.
   - Modified the description of the `escape` method in the SpEL context to indicate it now only takes one argument instead of two.
   - Updated an example query to use the new `escapeCharacter()` method instead of a hardcoded escape character.

3. **UserRepository.java**
   - Updated the query in the `findContainingEscaped` method to use `?#{escapeCharacter()}` instead of a hardcoded escape character (`'\\'`).

### Relationships Between Modifications

The modifications across the files are interconnected as follows:

- The changes in `EscapeCharacter.java` introduce a new method (`escapeCharacter()`) that allows access to the escape character, which is a key part of the functionality being documented and utilized in the other files.
- The documentation in `jpa.adoc` is updated to reflect the new method and its usage, ensuring that users of the API are aware of how to configure and use the escape character in their queries.
- The `UserRepository.java` file demonstrates the practical application of these changes by updating the query to utilize the new `escapeCharacter()` method, thereby ensuring that the escape character can be dynamically configured rather than being hardcoded. 

Overall, the modifications enhance the flexibility and usability of the escape character in JPA queries, while also ensuring that the documentation is consistent with the new API design.

Based on the commit message, the root cause and the patch provided below, you need to:

1. explain how the bug occurs, including all code statements that are most likely to lead to the bug and why. 
2. explain how the bug is fixed, including code statements fixing the bug and why.

Here is the commit message:
DATAJPA-1519 - Made the escapeCharacter itself available in
 SpEL expression.

Adapted documentation to reflect the changed API for escaping.

Here is the root cause:
The bug was caused by the lack of flexibility in specifying the escape character for LIKE queries, as the previous implementation required a hardcoded escape character in the query, which limited customization and could lead to incorrect behavior if the escape character needed to be changed. The new implementation allows the escape character to be dynamically configured and accessed through a method, improving usability and preventing potential errors in query execution.

Here is the patch:
 import java.util.List;
 
-import org.springframework.util.Assert;
+import org.springframework.lang.Nullable;
 
 /**
 	 * Escapes all special like characters ({@code _}, {@code %}) using the configured escape character.
 	 *
-	 * @param value must not be {@literal null}.
+	 * @param value May be {@literal null}.
 	 * @return
 	 */
+	@Nullable
 	public String escape(String value) {
 
-		Assert.notNull(value, "Value must be not null.");
-
+		if (value == null) {
+			return null;
+		}
 		return TO_REPLACE.stream() //
 				.reduce(value, (it, character) -> it.replace(character, this.value + character));
 	}
+
+	/**
+	 * Makes the underlying character available.
+	 *
+	 * @return the value
+	 */
+	public char escapeCharacter() {
+		return value;
+	}
 }
